message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Oral and upper respiratory microbiota play important roles in modulating host immune responses to viral infection. As emerging evidence suggests the host microbiome may be involved in the pathophysiology of COVID-19, we aimed to investigate associations between the oral and nasopharyngeal microbiome and COVID-19 severity. We collected saliva (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  = 78) and nasopharyngeal swab (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  = 66) samples from a COVID-19 cohort and characterized the microbiomes using 16S ribosomal RNA gene sequencing. We also examined associations between the salivary and nasopharyngeal microbiome and age, COVID-19 symptoms, and blood cytokines. SARS-CoV-2 infection status, but not COVID-19 severity, was associated with community-level differences in the oral and nasopharyngeal microbiomes. Salivary and nasopharyngeal microbiome alpha diversity negatively correlated with age and were associated with fever and diarrhea. Several bacterial genera were differentially abundant by COVID-19 severity, including oral
                  <jats:italic>Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus</jats:italic>
                  , and
                  <jats:italic>Solobacterium</jats:italic>
                  , all of which were depleted in patients with severe COVID-19. Nasopharyngeal
                  <jats:italic>Paracoccus</jats:italic>
                  was depleted while nasopharyngeal
                  <jats:italic>Proteus, Cupravidus, and Lactobacillus</jats:italic>
                  were increased in patients with severe COVID-19. Further analysis revealed that the abundance of oral
                  <jats:italic>Bifidobacterium</jats:italic>
                  was negatively associated with plasma concentrations of known COVID-19 biomarkers interleukin 17F (IL-17F) and monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1). In conclusion, our results suggest COVID-19 disease severity is associated with the relative abundance of certain bacterial taxa.
                </jats:p>
title: The salivary and nasopharyngeal microbiomes are associated with SARS-CoV-2 infection and disease severity
